Summary:
Systematic elaboration present.

In adult mice at homeostasis, Tfcp2l1 CreERT2 lineage tracing identified solitary SFTPC+/NKX2-1+ AT2 cells scattered throughout alveoli. The authors hypothesized that Nkx2-1 loss in AEPs is sufficient to induce a Krt8+ cell state. The authors suggested that Tfcp2l1 CreERT2 constitutes an AEP-enriched inducible epithelial Cre line suitable for epithelial-specific knockout of Nkx2-1. scRNAseq showed Tfcp2l1-lineage AT2 cells were molecularly indistinguishable from Axin2-positive AEPs and formed organoids indistinguishable from sorted Axin2 AEPs. Comparative transcriptional regulatory network analysis implicated Nkx2-1 as a central regulator of Wnt-responsive AT2 cells (AEPs) and transitional lineages. Genetic ablation of Nkx2-1 in AEPs in vitro caused irreversible acquisition of a stressed transitional state and uncontrolled proliferative growth. Nkx2-1 ablation in AEPs in vivo disrupted alveolar structure.
Reference: Toth, A., Kannan, P., Snowball, J., Kofron, M., Wayman, J. A., Bridges, J. P., Miraldi, E. R., Swarr, D., & Zacharias, W. J. (2023). Alveolar epithelial progenitor cells require Nkx2-1 to maintain progenitor-specific epigenomic state during lung homeostasis and regeneration. Nat Commun, 14(1), 8452. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-44184-0

Summary: In AT2 cells, CEBPA recruits NKX2-1 to CEBPA/NKX2-1 co-bound sites, promoting and maintaining the AT2 program. In AT2 cells, NKX2-1 binding and chromatin accessibility at CEBPA/NKX2-1 co-bound sites decrease more after Cebpa deletion than at NKX2-1 single-bound sites. This pattern supports that CEBPA recruits NKX2-1 to co-bound sites and that NKX2-1 single-bound sites are regulated by other factors. CEBPA-only sites have limited accessibility and limited NKX2-1 binding. CEBPA does not bind and thus indirectly represses sites that remain plastic in neonatal AT2 cells. In Cebpa mutant mature AT2 cells, NKX2-1 binding at AT2-specific peaks is decreased. NKX2-1 signal in purified Cebpa mutant AT2 cells increased. This increase possibly reflects limited redistribution to AT1-specific sites in the considerable number of HOPX low AT1-like cells. In the absence of CEBPA, neonatal but not mature AT2 cells have the plasticity to reactivate the SOX9 progenitor program.
Reference: Hassan, D., & Chen, J. (2024). CEBPA restricts alveolar type 2 cell plasticity during development and injury-repair. Nat Commun, 15(1), 4148. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-48632-3

Summary: Lin POS organoids were tested for differentiation into AT2 cells in medium containing DAPT, DCI, CHIR, and SB431542. In this condition NKX2-1 was upregulated alongside SFTPC and ACE2, and SOX9, SOX2, and TP63 were downregulated.
Reference: Lim, K., Donovan, A. P. A., Tang, W., Sun, D., He, P., Pett, J. P., Teichmann, S. A., Marioni, J. C., Meyer, K. B., Brand, A. H., & Rawlins, E. L. (2023). Organoid modeling of human fetal lung alveolar development reveals mechanisms of cell fate patterning and neonatal respiratory disease. Cell Stem Cell, 30(1), 20-37.e9. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.stem.2022.11.013

Summary: During perinatal and prenatal lung development, NKX2-1 acts as a transcription factor for AT2 cell transcriptional targets. NKX2-1 is required in AT2 cells to induce transcriptional programs that drive synthesis of surfactant lipids, surfactant proteins, and innate host‑defense proteins prior to birth. NKX2-1 directly controls genes encoding surfactant proteins A–D and lipid‑homeostasis genes. NKX2-1 activity is modulated by phosphorylation and by changes in chromatin accessibility. PRDM3 and PRDM16 control chromatin accessibility at NKX2-1 transcriptional targets critical for perinatal AT2 cell differentiation. Consequently, a dramatic increase in expression of NKX2-1 transcriptional targets occurs before birth without increased NKX2-1 expression. Combined deletion of Prdm3 and Prdm16 in early lung endoderm causes perinatal lethality from respiratory failure due to loss of AT2 cells and accumulation of partially differentiated AT1 cells. Lineage-specific deletion in AT2 cells leads to lineage infidelity with PRDM3/16-null cells acquiring partial AT1 fate.
Reference: He, H., Bell, S. M., Davis, A. K., Zhao, S., Sridharan, A., Na, C. L., Guo, M., Xu, Y., Snowball, J., Swarr, D. T., Zacharias, W. J., & Whitsett, J. A. (2024). PRDM3/16 regulate chromatin accessibility required for NKX2-1 mediated alveolar epithelial differentiation and function. Nat Commun, 15(1), 8112. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-52154-3
Hassan, D., & Chen, J. (2023). CEBPA restricts alveolar type 2 cell plasticity during development and injury-repair. Res Sq, , rs.3.rs-3521387. https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-3521387/v1
Note: No relevant passage was found in this paper.

Summary: NKX2-1 regulates respiratory epithelial cell fate decisions in the alveolar epithelium, including alveolar type I (AT1) and type II (AT2) cells. NKX2-1 functions within a network alongside YAP, TEAD, KLF5, and NFIB. NKX2-1 interacting partners change as AT1 and AT2 cells mature, and YAP partly regulates this partner shift in AT1 cells. Deletion of NKX2-1 in AT2 cells leads to loss of respiratory epithelial identity resembling gastrointestinal cells. Single-cell RNA sequencing of E18.5 fetal mouse lung during active AT1 and AT2 differentiation predicted NKX2-1 as a key regulator in both AT1 and AT2 cells.
Reference: Gokey, J. J., Snowball, J., Sridharan, A., Sudha, P., Kitzmiller, J. A., Xu, Y., & Whitsett, J. A. (2021). YAP regulates alveolar epithelial cell differentiation and <i>AGER</i> via NFIB/KLF5/NKX2-1. iScience, 24(9), 102967. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.isci.2021.102967
